citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Two rabbits were found in need of veterinary care at the time of this inspection conducted to follow up on two previous
consecutive severe animal welfare concerns for a brown and white rabbit. The condition affecting these rabbits were
either not identified or not communicated to the veterinarian:
• A tan and white rabbit was found with a dropped right ear with an internal surface that was uniformly red, thickened, and
inflamed. The surface of the skin was severely damaged with multiple ulcerated lesions creating an uneven ear margin
along the top and bottom edges. Dark red blood could be seen caked inside the inner folds as well as dark crusty raised
scab like material along the hairless top edge. The licensee stated they had identified this one rabbit with a bite wound on
its ear, but the veterinarian had not been notified.
• Then, a second tan rabbit was found hiding within a shelter with abnormal eyes. Both eyes were kept shut, oozing white
discharge that was weeping onto the skin surrounding the eyes. The entire area above, around, and below the left eye
was hairless, red, and inflamed with the exposed skin covered in ulcerated lesions and black crusty scab like material.
The right eyelid was thickened and reddened with crusty material caking around the eye. This condition had not been
identified by the licensee.
Failure to identify such conditions and to communicate findings with the veterinarian delays treatment and causes undue
animal pain and distress. A more rigorous method of daily observation of the rabbits must be implemented to properly
assess their health and well-being. Direct and frequent communication with the attending veterinarian is required for
timely diagnosis and adequate medical attention. The currently affected rabbits need to be seen by a veterinarian for an
appropriate diagnosis and treatment plan.

The rabbits housed in an open topped outdoor wire enclosure on the ground were showing signs of incompatibility. Per
the licensee, the rabbits fight occasionally and they recently identified one rabbit with bite wounds to the ear. At the time
of inspection, two rabbits were found with significant injuries that, per the licensee, were caused by fighting yet all the
rabbits were still housed together. A tan and white rabbit was found with ulcerated wounds to the majority of its right ear,
and a tan rabbit was found with discharge from both eyes as well as damage to the skin surrounding them. The licensee
also stated that the brown and white rabbit identified at the last inspection with a medical condition died subsequent to
fighting after being reintroduced. The incompatibility of the rabbits housed within this enclosure has resulted in injuries,
discomfort, and death. Animals housed in the same primary enclosure must be maintained in compatible groups.
Correct by: 04/01/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
Additional Inspectors:
STEPHANIE MAYARD, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Direct Repeat
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Two rabbits were found in need of veterinary care at the time of this inspection conducted to follow up on two previous 
consecutive severe animal welfare concerns for a brown and white rabbit. The condition affecting these rabbits were 
either not identified or not communicated to the veterinarian: 
• A tan and white rabbit was found with a dropped right ear with an internal surface that was uniformly red, thickened, and 
inflamed. The surface of the skin was severely damaged with multiple ulcerated lesions creating an uneven ear margin 
along the top and bottom edges. Dark red blood could be seen caked inside the inner folds as well as dark crusty raised 
scab like material along the hairless top edge. The licensee stated they had identified this one rabbit with a bite wound on 
its ear, but the veterinarian had not been notified. 
• Then, a second tan rabbit was found hiding within a shelter with abnormal eyes. Both eyes were kept shut, oozing white 
discharge that was weeping onto the skin surrounding the eyes. The entire area above, around, and below the left eye 
was hairless, red, and inflamed with the exposed skin covered in ulcerated lesions and black crusty scab like material. 
The right eyelid was thickened and reddened with crusty material caking around the eye. This condition had not been 
identified by the licensee. 
Failure to identify such conditions and to communicate findings with the veterinarian delays treatment and causes undue 
animal pain and distress. A more rigorous method of daily observation of the rabbits must be implemented to properly 
assess their health and well-being. Direct and frequent communication with the attending veterinarian is required for 
timely diagnosis and adequate medical attention. The currently affected rabbits need to be seen by a veterinarian for an 
appropriate diagnosis and treatment plan. 
3.58  Direct
   
Classification and separation. 
The rabbits housed in an open topped outdoor wire enclosure on the ground were showing signs of incompatibility. Per 
the licensee, the rabbits fight occasionally and they recently identified one rabbit with bite wounds to the ear. At the time 
of inspection, two rabbits were found with significant injuries that, per the licensee, were caused by fighting yet all the 
rabbits were still housed together. A tan and white rabbit was found with ulcerated wounds to the majority of its right ear, 
and a tan rabbit was found with discharge from both eyes as well as damage to the skin surrounding them. The licensee 
also stated that the brown and white rabbit identified at the last inspection with a medical condition died subsequent to 
fighting after being reintroduced. The incompatibility of the rabbits housed within this enclosure has resulted in injuries,

discomfort, and death. Animals housed in the same primary enclosure must be maintained in compatible groups. 
Correct by: 04/01/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
STEPHANIE MAYARD, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
